key_insight: In compressed crystalline HCP titanium, elastic response is size- and rate-independent, but plastic deformation strongly depends on both system size and strain rate.
engineering_relevance: For reliable atomistic predictions of titanium plasticity, simulations must use sufficiently large models and low enough strain rates to capture stable dislocation-mediated behavior.
method: Molecular-dynamics simulations of alpha-titanium under uniaxial compression.
System size varied across four orders of magnitude, up to 32 million atoms.
Strain rates tested down to 10^8 s^-1 to probe size–rate coupling.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that elastic properties stayed constant, while larger systems reduced stress fluctuations, produced more homogeneous evolution, and stabilized dislocation activity during plastic flow.
watch_item: Very small cells or too-high strain rates can bias plasticity results, so size and rate must be co-designed to avoid non-equilibrated artifacts.
paper_title: Uniaxial compression of crystalline HCP titanium: an atomistic modelling study of size effects
